title: Population
facts: India passed China to become the world's most populous country around 2023.
More than half the world's population lives in Asia.

title: GDP (Nominal)
facts: The world's GDP is heavily concentrated in a small number of countries.
Nominal GDP measures economic size, not how wealthy individual citizens are.

title: Coffee Production
facts: Brazil alone accounts for roughly a third of the world's coffee production.
Vietnam is the world's largest producer of robusta beans.
Ethiopia is widely considered the birthplace of coffee.

title: Forest Area (% of land)
facts: Tropical rainforests play an outsized role in global carbon absorption.
Forest cover varies enormously with a country's climate and terrain.

title: Life Expectancy
facts: Life expectancy is shaped by healthcare access, nutrition, and public health systems.
Japan has one of the highest life expectancies in the world.

title: Olympic Medals (All-time)
facts: The USA holds the most all-time Olympic medals of any nation.
Medal counts are influenced by population size, wealth, and number of Games attended.

title: Coconut Production
facts: Indonesia alone accounts for roughly a quarter of the world's coconut production.
Indonesia, the Philippines, and India together produce around 70% of the world's coconuts.

title: Tea Production
facts: China accounts for roughly half of the world's tea production.
Kenya is one of the world's largest tea exporters despite a much smaller population than China or India.

title: Oil Production
facts: The United States has been the world's largest crude oil producer for several years running.
Saudi Arabia can adjust its oil output significantly to influence global prices as the leader of OPEC+.

title: Active Volcanoes
facts: Indonesia, the United States, Japan, and Chile have the most volcanoes active during the last 12,000 years.
Iceland sits directly on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, giving it an unusually high volcano count for its small size.

title: Rice Production
facts: India and China together grow more rice than the rest of the world combined.
Rice requires a warm, humid climate, which is why production is overwhelmingly concentrated in Asia.

title: Cocoa Production
facts: Ivory Coast alone produces more than a third of the world's cocoa, though it isn't in this roster.
Indonesia is the largest cocoa producer outside West Africa.

title: Fish & Seafood Production
facts: China produces more fish and seafood than the next several largest producers combined.
Norway's aquaculture industry, mostly farmed salmon, punches far above its population size.

title: Sheep Population
facts: China has more sheep than any other country, at roughly twice the number of second-place India.
Iceland has more sheep than people, despite not cracking the global top ranks by raw count.

title: Gold Production
facts: China has been the world's largest gold-mining country for years running.
Australia holds some of the largest known gold reserves on Earth in addition to its high production.

title: Natural Gas Production
facts: The United States produces more natural gas than any other country by a wide margin.
Norway became one of Europe's most important gas suppliers after other supply routes were disrupted.

title: Renewable Energy Share
facts: Norway generates the overwhelming majority of its electricity from hydropower.
Countries with a lot of traditional biomass use for cooking and heating can show a surprisingly high renewable share on paper.

title: CO2 Emissions per Capita
facts: Per-capita emissions and total national emissions tell very different stories — a small, wealthy oil producer can outrank a much larger country.
Ethiopia's per-capita emissions are among the lowest in the world.

title: Internet Users
facts: Several small, wealthy countries report internet usage rates near-universal across their population.
Internet access gaps remain largest in lower-income countries, even where mobile phone ownership is common.

title: International Tourist Arrivals
facts: France was the first country ever to surpass 100 million annual international tourist arrivals.
Turkey draws more international visitors each year than countries with far larger populations.

title: Copper Production
facts: Chile alone produces roughly a quarter of the world's copper.
Chile is home to the two largest copper mines on Earth, Escondida and Collahuasi.

title: World Happiness Score
facts: Nordic countries have dominated the top of the World Happiness Report for over a decade.
The ranking is based on self-reported life evaluation surveys, not income or GDP alone.

title: Number of Billionaires
facts: The United States and China together are home to nearly half of the world's billionaires.
Billionaire counts can shift quickly year to year with stock market swings.

title: Military Expenditure
facts: The United States spends more on its military than the next several highest-spending countries combined.
Military spending doesn't always track population size — several oil-producing nations spend heavily relative to their size.

title: Wine Production
facts: Chile and Australia are both major wine producers despite being far from Europe's traditional wine regions.
The United States is one of the largest wine producers outside of Europe, led by California.

title: Wheat Production
facts: China and India together grow roughly a third of the world's wheat.
Turkey's wheat harvest is large enough to rank among the world's top ten producers.

title: Banana Production
facts: India grows more bananas than any other country, though most are consumed domestically rather than exported.
The Philippines is one of the world's largest banana exporters.

title: Wool Production
facts: Australia produces more wool than any other country, tied closely to its enormous sheep flock.
China's wool output is boosted by one of the largest sheep populations on Earth.

title: UNESCO World Heritage Sites
facts: China and Germany rank among the countries with the most UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
A country's site count doesn't always track its population — smaller nations with deep histories can rank surprisingly high.

title: Coastline Length
facts: Indonesia's roughly 17,000 islands give it one of the longest coastlines on Earth.
Some countries in this game, like Ethiopia and Switzerland, are landlocked and have no coastline at all.
